title: Fanny’s First Play
description: Fanny’s First Play — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.description: "Table of contents for Fanny's First Play"

memes.label: The Adelphi
memes.description: The London district on the Strand where the Little Theatre was located — the venue of Fanny's First Play's premiere on April 19, 1911.

memes.label: Adulthood
memes.description: The transition from dependent childhood to independent adulthood — achieved in Fanny's First Play not through age but through moral crisis and self-discovery.

memes.label: Adventure
memes.description: Adventure as moral testing ground in Fanny's First Play — Bobby's fortnight away, Margaret's night in prison, and Dora's perpetual hustle are adventures that the respectable world can only interpret as delinquency.

memes.label: Ambition
memes.description: Ambition and its absence in the Gilbey-Knox world — the characters' varying relationships to social climbing, professional achievement, and the desire for something more than respectability.

memes.label: Appearance and Reality
memes.description: The gap between appearance and reality as Shaw's central dramatic method — critics who judge by appearances, parents who maintain appearances, and children who expose the reality beneath.

memes.label: Aristotle
memes.description: Aristotle as invoked in Shaw's critical satire — the "Stagirite" appears as shorthand for classical dramatic authority, whose rules the modern play both obeys and subverts.

memes.label: Art and Theatre
memes.description: The function of dramatic art as debated in Shaw's play — is theatre entertainment, moral instruction, or truth-telling? The Induction's critical debate and the inner play's performance together argue for art as the exposure of uncomfortable truths.

memes.label: Authority
memes.description: Parental authority, critical authority, and moral authority — all challenged, subverted, or exposed as hollow in the play. Shaw dramatizes the collapse of traditional authority structures and asks what, if anything, should replace them.

memes.label: Authorship
memes.description: The question of who wrote the play — within the play and outside it — as Shaw's experiment in anonymous criticism, testing whether a work can be judged on its merits rather than its author's reputation.

memes.label: Bannal
memes.description: Flawner Bannal's philosophy of the commonplace — the idea that a play's merit is determined solely by its author's reputation, representing the unreflective consumer's approach to art that Shaw satirizes through the youngest critic in the Induction.

memes.label: Beauty
memes.description: Count O'Dowda's aestheticism — the pursuit of beauty as an escape from vulgarity, a substitute for engagement with the modern world, and a form of aristocratic withdrawal that the inner play's rough vitality implicitly critiques.

memes.label: Bobby Gilbey
memes.description: The son of the Gilbey household whose imprisonment for assaulting a policeman drives the inner play's plot — a reluctant rebel who discovers freedom in disgrace and honesty in the admission that he is "a gaol-bird."

memes.label: Brighton
memes.description: The seaside resort that functions in Act II and Act III as a cover story and euphemism — the Gilbeys claim Bobby is "in Brighton for the sea air" when he is in fact in prison, turning the town's reputation for pleasure into a symbol of respectable deception.

memes.label: Business
memes.description: The world of commerce and trade that anchors the Gilbey and Knox families — the economic foundation of respectability in Fanny's First Play.

memes.label: Byron
memes.description: Lord Byron as cultural reference in Shaw's play — the Byronic ideal of romantic rebellion, contrasted with the play's more prosaic and domestic version of youthful defiance.

memes.label: Camberwell
memes.description: A south London working-class district, contrasted with the more aspirational Denmark Hill in Shaw's "Fanny's First Play" — representing the class geography of London's southern suburbs in the early twentieth century.

memes.label: Cambridge Fabian Society
memes.description: The university branch of the Fabian Society referenced in Shaw's Preface, representing the intellectual milieu that shaped the play's ideas

memes.label: Cambridge University
memes.description: The ancient university where Fanny O'Dowda encountered the Fabian Society and modern intellectual life — the invisible engine of the play's plot, whose transformation of the Count's daughter sets the entire Induction in motion.

memes.label: Cecil Savoyard
memes.description: The actor-manager engaged by Fanny O'Dowda to produce her anonymous play and assemble a team of critics — a worldly-wise theatrical professional who serves as the practical bridge between art and commerce.

memes.label: Censorship and Art
memes.description: The tension between creative freedom and social-moral constraint that drives both the play's frame narrative and its thematic argument — Shaw's dramatization of art's perpetual conflict with the respectable order that would suppress it.

memes.label: Character
memes.description: Character as moral quality — "good character" as a social certification versus actual goodness. The play repeatedly tests the meaning of "having a character" and finds it wanting as a measure of genuine moral worth.

memes.label: Church of England
memes.description: The established church whose moral authority is quietly but persistently undermined throughout Shaw's play

memes.label: Class
memes.description: The Edwardian class system as satirized in Shaw's play — where social position is simultaneously an obsession, a prison, and a performance that characters maintain at the cost of their integrity.

memes.label: Class Consciousness
memes.description: The characters' varying awareness of class — from the Gilbeys' anxious respectability to the Count's aristocratic ease to Dora's cheerful transcendence of class categories.

memes.label: Comedy
memes.description: Comedy as Shaw's chosen mode in Fanny's First Play — not mere entertainment but a critical method, a way of exposing truths that tragedy would ennoble and melodrama would sentimentalize.

memes.label: Confession
memes.description: The act of honest self-disclosure that structures the moral climax of Fanny's First Play — Bobby and Margaret's mutual confessions of imprisonment, and the broader Shavian argument that truth-telling is the foundation of genuine morality.

memes.label: Convention
memes.description: The unwritten rules of social life — the customs and habits that Shaw identified as the enemy of genuine moral development in Fanny's First Play.

memes.label: Count O'Dowda
memes.description: The aristocratic Irish father of Fanny O'Dowda, an aesthete who fled industrial England for Venice — a portrait of the artist-manqué who values beauty over truth and discovers that Cambridge has undone his carefully cultivated paradise.

memes.label: Country and Nation
memes.description: National identity and its discontents in Shaw's play — Englishness, Irishness, Frenchness, and Italianness as competing claims on identity, with Duvallet's comic Francophilia exposing the contradictions of patriotism.

memes.label: Courage
memes.description: The moral courage required to defy social convention — a central value in Shaw's ethical vision as expressed through the characters of Fanny's First Play.

memes.label: Crime and Punishment
memes.description: The social and moral dimensions of crime, imprisonment, and punishment in Shaw's play — where going to prison becomes a rite of passage that exposes the hypocrisy of respectable society.

memes.label: Dance
memes.description: Dancing as liberation and self-expression in Fanny's First Play — Margaret's transformative night of dancing, the stage dancer she admires, and dance as a metaphor for the freedom the young characters seek.

memes.label: Denmark Hill
memes.description: The respectable south London suburb where the Gilbey and Knox families live — a neighborhood whose very name signifies middle-class aspiration, religious propriety, and the social performance that Shaw's inner play systematically dismantles.

memes.label: Dora Delaney
memes.description: The "hardened" young woman whose arrest is reported under the headline "Hardened at Eighteen" — unashamed, unsentimental, and more morally clear-sighted than the respectable characters who condemn her.

memes.label: Doubt
memes.description: Religious and moral doubt in Fanny's First Play — from Knox's admission that he has no real faith to Margaret's honest doubt about her parents' moral authority, doubt appears as intellectual honesty rather than moral failure.

memes.label: Dress and Costume
memes.description: Costume as social statement in Fanny's First Play — from the Count's obsolete Venetian dress to Juggins's Spanish livery, clothing marks class allegiance, aesthetic philosophy, and theatrical role-playing.

memes.label: Duty
memes.description: The moral obligation to family, society, and self — a contested concept in Fanny's First Play, where duty to convention often conflicts with duty to conscience.

memes.label: Lieutenant Duvallet
memes.description: A French naval officer who becomes embroiled in Margaret Knox's arrest after the boat-race night brawl — Shaw's gentleman-adventurer who breaches English propriety with Continental charm

memes.label: Education
memes.description: Formal and informal education as depicted in Shaw's play — Cambridge as intellectual pretension, the street as moral education, and the failure of respectable upbringing to produce genuine character.

memes.label: Engagement
memes.description: The social institution of betrothal as depicted in Fanny's First Play — a "respectable" arrangement that Bobby tries desperately to escape, only to discover that the rules of engagement, like the rules of respectability itself, offer no honorable exit.

memes.label: England
memes.description: The national setting of the inner play, whose institutions and class structure Shaw subjects to sustained satirical scrutiny

memes.label: Experience
memes.description: The Shavian argument that direct, often transgressive, lived experience is a superior moral teacher than abstract authority, convention, or inherited wisdom — dramatised through Margaret Knox's night in prison and her subsequent transformation.

memes.label: Fabian Society
memes.description: The gradualist socialist organization founded in 1884 to which Shaw belonged — the intellectual engine of Edwardian progressivism that appears in the play as the Cambridge society that transformed Fanny O'Dowda and gave her the analytical tools to confound the critics.

memes.label: Failure
memes.description: Failure as liberation in Shaw's comic vision — the parents' fear of failure (social, moral, parental) is contrasted with the young characters' discovery that surviving failure is the beginning of freedom.

memes.label: Family
memes.description: The middle-class family as depicted in Shaw's play — an institution that simultaneously protects and imprisons its members, with parental authority exposed as both comic and tragic in its inadequacy.

memes.label: Fanny O'Dowda
memes.description: The nineteen-year-old Cambridge-educated author of the play-within-the-play — Shaw's portrait of the artist as innocent, whose work confounds the critics who cannot judge without the author's name, and whose education has estranged her from her father's eighteenth-century aesthetic world.

memes.label: Flawner Bannal
memes.description: The youngest of the four critics in the Induction and Epilogue, representing the "man in the street" — a twenty-year-old unemployable of the business class whose cheerful ignorance and commercial instinct make him, according to Savoyard, the most reliable barometer of public taste.

memes.label: Florence
memes.description: Count O'Dowda's aesthetic ideal — the Italian city that represents his vision of beauty, art, and aristocratic withdrawal from modern England, invoked as an "earthly paradise" achievable on 1500 pounds a year.

memes.label: Florence Towers
memes.description: Count O'Dowda's country house — an "old-fashioned" estate that serves as the theatrical frame for both the Induction and Epilogue of Fanny's First Play.

memes.label: Footman
memes.description: Count O'Dowda's footman at Florence Towers, dressed in "grandiose Spanish livery" — a silent frame character whose single scene of announcing the critics establishes the theatrical ritual of the Induction and whose servant's perspective subtly undermines the pretensions of his employer.

memes.label: Forgiveness
memes.description: The act of pardoning transgression — a concept Shaw complicates by showing that forgiveness can be a form of moral blackmail in Fanny's First Play.

memes.label: Freedom
memes.description: The condition the young characters achieve through transgression — not political liberty but the moral freedom that comes from shedding the pretense of respectability and discovering that life is possible without it.

memes.label: Freedom of Speech
memes.description: Margaret Knox's discovery of a new language — the words she ''didn't even know that I knew'' — as the most intimate form of free speech, breaking the linguistic cage of respectability.

memes.label: "Gender Roles"
memes.description: "The tension between traditional feminine expectations and the emerging independence of the 'New Woman' in Edwardian England, as explored through Fanny O'Dowda, Margaret Knox, and Dora Delaney."

memes.label: Generational Conflict
memes.description: The conflict between parents and children in Shaw's play — not merely domestic comedy but a philosophical clash between the morality of custom and the morality of experience, with the younger generation winning by going to prison.

memes.label: Gentleman
memes.description: The ideal of gentlemanly conduct — courtesy, honour, and self-control — that serves as both a social standard and a satirical target in Fanny's First Play.

memes.label: Mr. Robin Gilbey
memes.description: Father of Bobby Gilbey and husband to Maria, a respectable shopkeeper whose horror at his son's social disgrace reveals the fragility of lower-middle-class status

memes.label: God
memes.description: The divine as invoked, questioned, and reimagined throughout Fanny's First Play — from conventional piety to the Life Force that Shaw proposed as religion's modern successor.

memes.label: Harley Granville Barker
memes.description: The influential Edwardian director, playwright, and theatre reformer whose name is invoked in the Epilogue as a benchmark of quality — Gunn attributes the inner play to him, and Fanny is "radiant" at the comparison.

memes.label: Guilt
memes.description: The moral framework of sin, blame, and punishment that the older generation imposes on the young in Fanny's First Play — a framework that Shaw systematically dismantles in favour of a morality based on self-knowledge rather than social condemnation.

memes.label: Mr. Gunn
memes.description: A minor character in the Induction, one of the critics judging Fanny's play — Shaw's sketch of the self-important cultural gatekeeper

memes.label: Happiness
memes.description: What constitutes happiness? The characters debate this central question — Mrs. Knox's philosophy of "happiness within" versus external respectability, and Count O'Dowda's aesthetic paradise versus the rough vitality of the inner play — forming the philosophical core of Shaw's moral argument.

memes.label: Heaven
memes.description: The religious concept of heaven — invoked ironically and earnestly throughout Fanny's First Play — as a foil to the earthly concerns of respectability and social judgment.

memes.label: Hell
memes.description: The rhetorical and theological concept of hell — invoked as a curse, a metaphor, and a genuine moral reality — in Fanny's First Play.

memes.label: Holloway Gaol
memes.description: The London women's prison where Margaret Knox spends her fortnight — the crucible in which her moral awakening occurs, and the central symbol of the play's argument that respectability is a cage and disgrace can be a liberation.

memes.label: Home
memes.description: The domestic sphere as both prison and haven in Fanny's First Play — the Gilbey and Knox homes embody respectability, comfort, and suffocation, while the young characters find liberation only by leaving home, literally and morally.

memes.label: Hope
memes.description: Hope as a theme in Shaw's play — from Fanny's anxious hope that her play succeeds to the Count's hope for a world of beauty, hope appears as both aspiration and delusion, driving action while sometimes obscuring reality.

memes.label: Hypocrisy
memes.description: The central moral failing exposed by Shaw's play — the gap between respectable appearance and actual conduct, between moral profession and moral practice, which the play's structure of exposure systematically dismantles.

memes.label: Identity
memes.description: The question of who characters really are — beneath their social roles, family expectations, and public performances — explored through concealed authorship, disguised nobility, and the transformative discovery of self-knowledge in Fanny's First Play.

memes.label: Ignorance
memes.description: The willed not-knowing of the respectable older generation — what parents do not know and do not want to know about their children's real lives, and the Shavian argument that this ignorance is a moral failure, not innocence.

memes.label: Illusion
memes.description: The layered confusions between appearance and reality that structure Fanny's First Play — from the concealed authorship to the critics' inability to distinguish fiction from autobiography, and the older generation's willed blindness to their children's real lives.

memes.label: Individuality
memes.description: The assertion of the self against social conformity — the value Shaw champions above all others in Fanny's First Play.

memes.label: Innocence
memes.description: Innocence redefined in Shaw's play — not the absence of experience but the refusal to be shamed by it, as Margaret insists her night of dancing was 'innocent' despite her parents' horror.

memes.label: Judgment
memes.description: The critics' judgment of the play, the parents' moral judgment of their children, and the final judgment that the play itself delivers on respectable society — judgment is both a theme and a structural principle throughout the play.

memes.label: Juggins
memes.description: The footman of the Gilbey household whose mysterious origins, moral authority, and strategic silences make him the play's most enigmatic figure — possibly the brother of a duke, certainly the only character who understands everyone else better than they understand themselves.

memes.label: Knowledge
memes.description: The pursuit of understanding — through education, experience, and transgression — as a path to moral awakening in Fanny's First Play.

memes.label: Joseph Knox
memes.description: The respectable shopkeeper and father of Margaret Knox whose enforcement of morality on others collapses when his own daughter goes to prison — the play's most devastating portrait of structural hypocrisy and the moral bankruptcy of "mere morality."

memes.label: Lady
memes.description: The social ideal of womanhood — the "lady" as a figure of respectability, moral guardianship, and gendered constraint in Fanny's First Play.

memes.label: Lady Constance Lytton
memes.description: The suffragette leader with whom Fanny O'Dowda served a month in prison — a real historical figure whose name reveals Fanny's political awakening and her break from her father's aesthetic world.

memes.label: Language
memes.description: Language as class marker and dramatic tool in Fanny's First Play — from Cockney dialect to French accent to the specialized vocabularies of criticism and religion, language reveals social position and shapes what can be thought.

memes.label: Little Theatre
memes.description: The London venue in the Adelphi where Fanny's First Play premiered on April 19, 1911 — a site of experimental drama representing the insurgent theatrical culture that challenged the commercial West End and embodied the repertory movement Shaw championed.

memes.label: London
memes.description: The city that contains the play's multiple social worlds — from the Little Theatre in the Adelphi to the West Circular Road suburbs, from Piccadilly Circus to the variety theatres — Shaw's map of Edwardian class geography inscribed in brick and street names.

memes.label: Love
memes.description: Romantic love, courtship, and marriage in the play — from Bobby and Margaret's conventional engagement to Dora's "unwomanly" independence from romantic convention, love is treated by Shaw as both a genuine human bond and a social institution ripe for satire.

memes.label: Manners
memes.description: Social manners as both a weapon and a mask in Fanny's First Play — Dora's deft manipulation of genteel speech to disarm a policeman, the critics' varying codes of politeness, and the sharp distinction between genuine courtesy and class-bound etiquette.

memes.label: Margaret Knox
memes.description: The daughter of the Knox household whose imprisonment transforms her from a prize-winning schoolgirl into the play's philosopher of freedom — she articulates the Shavian thesis that goodness without freedom is servitude.

memes.label: Marriage
memes.description: The institution of marriage as depicted in Shaw's play — simultaneously a social contract, a moral obligation, and a target of satirical critique in Edwardian society.

memes.label: Melodrama
memes.description: Melodrama as the theatrical mode Shaw defines himself against — the well-made play, the sensational curtain, the moralized ending that Fanny's First Play subverts at every turn.

memes.label: Modernity
memes.description: The clash between traditional values and modern ideas — the central cultural tension of Fanny's First Play, embodied in the conflict between the Count's eighteenth-century aestheticism and the modern world.

memes.label: Money
memes.description: Financial considerations in the Gilbey/Knox world — the economics of respectability, the cost of freedom, and the role of money in defining social standing, marriage prospects, and moral worth in Edwardian middle-class society.

memes.label: Morality
memes.description: The distinction Shaw draws between "mere morality" — the substitution of custom for conscience — and genuine ethical reflection, which may require the loss of social respectability.

memes.label: Maria Gilbey
memes.description: The anxious guardian of the Gilbey household's respectability — a woman whose moral vigilance produces a son who goes to prison and whose discovery that "we could live without being respectable" is the parental generation's most startling revelation.

memes.label: Amelia Knox
memes.description: The wife of Joseph Knox whose insistence that "he ought to marry her whether or no" represents the parental generation's reductive moral arithmetic — marriage as the universal solvent for moral problems.

memes.label: Music
memes.description: Music as theme and metaphor in Fanny's First Play — from the Count's classical aestheticism to Margaret's hymn-singing and Dora's concertina, music marks class divisions and the yearning for a fuller life.

memes.label: "National Identity"
memes.description: "The satirical treatment of English, Irish, Italian, and French national character in Fanny's First Play, with particular focus on Shaw's ironic deployment of the outsider's perspective."

memes.label: Nature
memes.description: The tension between human nature and social convention in Fanny's First Play — what is "natural" (genuine impulse, authentic selfhood) versus what society deems acceptable, explored through Margaret's claim that she acted "out of the very depths of my nature" and Shaw's insistence that respectability is itself "unnatural."

memes.label: Noise
memes.description: Noise and disorder in Fanny's First Play — from the "Cockney twang" that offends the Count to the concertina's music that signals liberation, noise is the sound of life breaking through the walls of respectability.

memes.label: Oxford
memes.description: The ancient university town deployed by Trotter in the Induction as a rhetorical weapon in the Oxford-Cambridge rivalry, and later invoked in Act II through its students whose Boat Race rowdiness triggers the police violence that lands Margaret in Holloway.

memes.label: Paris
memes.description: The French capital invoked in the Induction as the cosmopolitan ideal — a city associated with wit, sophistication, and a refreshing contrast to English provincialism, embodied by Trotter and the French character of Duvallet.

memes.label: Passion
memes.description: Passion versus propriety — Dora's vitality, Margaret's rebellion, and the critics' debate about whether the play contains "the note of passion." Shaw affirms passion as a life-force that respectability seeks to suppress but cannot extinguish.

memes.label: Performance
memes.description: The act of performing — on stage and in life — as a central motif in Fanny's First Play, where theatrical performance and social performance mirror and critique each other.

memes.label: "Philistinism"
memes.description: "A central satirical target in Fanny's First Play: the cultural and moral complacency of Edwardian middle-class England, as embodied in the conflict between aesthetic idealism and industrial modernity."

memes.label: Arthur Wing Pinero
memes.description: The commercially successful Edwardian playwright repeatedly invoked in the Epilogue as a benchmark — the critics attribute Fanny's anonymous play to him, making him a key figure in Shaw's satire of critical judgment.

memes.label: Pleasure
memes.description: Pleasure and its discontents in Shaw's play — from Mrs. Gilbey's "two evenings pleasure every week" to Margaret's ecstatic night of dancing, the play contrasts respectable, scheduled pleasure with the spontaneous joy that shatters convention.

memes.label: Prison
memes.description: Imprisonment as moral education — a central paradox of Fanny's First Play, where the experience of arrest and jail proves more formative than church, school, or family.

memes.label: Prison
memes.description: The off-stage prison where Bobby Gilbey, Margaret Knox, and Dora Delaney are held — the play's paradoxical site of moral education, where characters discover the freedom that respectability denies them.

memes.label: Profession and Occupation
memes.description: Professional identity in Fanny's First Play — the critics as professionals, the Gilbey-Knox business world, and the way occupation shapes character and consciousness.

memes.label: Reason
memes.description: Reason versus emotion, reason as Enlightenment value, Shaw's faith in reason as the foundation of moral and social progress — and the play's comic exposure of how rarely reason actually governs human behavior.

memes.label: Religion
memes.description: The role of organized religion and personal piety in Edwardian society as satirized in Shaw's play — a social credential rather than a spiritual conviction.

memes.label: Reputation
memes.description: Reputation as the currency of the respectable world — the Gilbeys' and Knoxes' obsessive concern with what others think, contrasted with the young characters' discovery that reputation, once lost, ceases to matter.

memes.label: Respect
memes.description: Respectability and self-respect — the Gilbey/Knox obsession with being respected by others versus the integrity of self-respect that Shaw argues is the only genuine foundation for moral life. A key distinction that structures the play's moral argument.

memes.label: Respectability
memes.description: The social performance of moral uprightness in Edwardian society, critiqued by Shaw as a hollow substitute for genuine conscience — the central satirical target of the inner play and the thematic bridge between the Induction's critics and the Gilbey-Knox world.

memes.label: Cecil Savoyard
memes.description: The Savoyard actor-manager who brings Fanny's play to the stage — Shaw's affectionate portrait of the theatrical professional who serves art rather than morality

memes.label: Scandal
memes.description: Scandal as social weapon and moral test in Fanny's First Play — the fear of scandal drives the parents' responses, while the young characters discover that surviving scandal is liberating.

memes.label: Secrecy
memes.description: The concealment of truth — of authorship, of arrest, of identity — as both a dramatic device and a moral theme in Fanny's First Play.

memes.label: Self-Knowledge
memes.description: The journey from self-ignorance to self-knowledge that defines the play's young characters — Margaret's discovery that she is not ''the sort of girl I thought myself'' and Bobby's parallel awakening.

memes.label: Self-Sacrifice
memes.description: The ideal of self-sacrifice as critiqued in Fanny's First Play — Juggins exposes it as a "romantic luxury" that women indulge in when it coincides with their desires, making it a tool of self-deception rather than genuine moral action.

memes.label: Servants
memes.description: The servant class in the play — Juggins as the knowing servant who subverts the master-servant relationship, the footman as social observer, and the institution of domestic service as a lens through which Shaw examines class, deference, and the performance of social roles.

memes.label: Shakespeare
memes.description: Shakespeare as the inescapable reference point for Edwardian drama — Shaw's ambivalent relationship to the Shakespearean tradition, invoked in the play's critical debate as both model and obstacle.

memes.label: Shame
memes.description: The fear of public exposure and loss of social standing that drives the moral economy of Edwardian respectability — contrasted by Shaw with genuine conscience, self-respect, and the liberating effect of acknowledged disgrace.

memes.label: George Bernard Shaw
memes.description: The author of "Fanny's First Play" (1911), who appears in the Preface as commentator and self-satirist — his presence as implied author permeates the play's meta-theatrical frame and critical dialogue.

memes.label: Silence
memes.description: Strategic and dramatic silence in Shaw's play — the silences that speak louder than words, from the "dead silence" that follows Knox's commands to the critics' stunned silence at the play's conclusion.

memes.label: Sincerity
memes.description: Sincerity as moral value in Shaw's play — the young characters' insistence on saying what they mean (Margaret's refusal to apologize, Dora's cheerful honesty) against the evasions of respectable language.

memes.label: Social Suicide
memes.description: The loss of respectability — a kind of social death that Shaw presents not as catastrophe but as liberation in Fanny's First Play.

memes.label: Society
memes.description: The collective social order that enforces respectability and conformity — Shaw's primary satirical target in Fanny's First Play.

memes.label: Solitude
memes.description: Solitude and isolation in Fanny's First Play — the Count's chosen solitude in Venice, Margaret's enforced solitude in prison, and the existential solitude of characters who can no longer share their parents' values.

memes.label: The Stage
memes.description: The theatrical stage as both physical space and metaphor in Fanny's First Play — the site of performance, judgment, and truth-telling.

memes.label: Style
memes.description: Literary and personal style in Fanny's First Play — the critics' varying prose styles, the Count's aesthetic style, and Shaw's own stylistic performance as playwright-critic.

memes.label: Success
memes.description: What constitutes success? The play's commercial and critical reception in the frame story versus the inner play's characters' achievements — success as a concept that Shaw treats with characteristic irony, exposing the gap between worldly success and genuine accomplishment.

memes.label: Theatre
memes.description: The art form of theatrical performance as it appears in Shaw's "Fanny's First Play" — both the medium of the inner play and the subject of critical debate in the Induction frame, where definitions of what constitutes a "real play" are contested.

memes.label: Theatre Criticism
memes.description: Shaw's sustained satire of dramatic criticism as practised by the Edwardian establishment — the critic as unwitting subject of the play he judges

memes.label: Tradition
memes.description: The inherited customs and values that Shaw challenges in Fanny's First Play — the weight of the past against the demands of the present.

memes.label: Tradition versus Modernity
memes.description: The central cultural conflict of the play — the Count's eighteenth-century aestheticism versus the modern world of motor-cars, music halls, and suffragettes.

memes.label: Mr. Trotter
memes.description: The gentleman-amateur critic in the Induction who judges plays by their propriety — Shaw's lampoon of the critic who cannot recognize himself in satire

memes.label: Truth
memes.description: The value that Shaw's play places above respectability — the truth-telling that exposes hypocrisy, the honesty of the "gaol-bird" over the respectable citizen, and the uncomfortable truths that the theatre can tell when freed from critical orthodoxy.

memes.label: Mr. Vaughan
memes.description: An intellectual critic in the Induction, more thoughtful than Trotter but equally blind to the satire directed at his class

memes.label: Venice
memes.description: Count O'Dowda's aesthetic paradise and retreat — an idealized eighteenth-century Venice of palazzi, gondolas, and aristocratic taste, invoked throughout the Induction and Epilogue as the antithesis of modern England and the destination to which the Count flees when confronted with his daughter's play.

memes.label: West Circular Road
memes.description: The suburban address of the Knox family, whose respectability is performed through location as much as behaviour

memes.label: Writing
memes.description: The act of writing as theme in Fanny's First Play — Fanny as playwright, the critics as writers, Shaw as authorial presence, and writing as the means by which young women claim public authority.

memes.label: Youth and Age
memes.description: The generational divide that structures the play — the young characters' vitality and honesty against the old characters' rigidity and self-deception.
